reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The employer and tech-sector interest in labour-market access, skills alignment, and early identification of talent-at-scale; the public-good frame is efficient human-capital allocation and reduced barriers to entry for high-ability candidates regardless of family background.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector who depend on a pipeline of analytically strong, adaptable, early-identified talent across the full ability range.
reactions.public_good_frame: A standardised, transparent admission process that identifies genuine analytical capability early, reducing information asymmetry and enabling employers to build recruitment relationships with schools that serve diverse SES backgrounds — expanding the talent pool and reducing future skills-matching friction.
reactions.argument: S1 narrows rather than solves the problem. A standardised central admission test at 10/12 is an improvement over teacher gatekeeping — it is more predictable, auditable, and reduces coaching-arms-race noise. However, S1 leaves the 6/8-year track structure intact and reaches only ~8–12% of the cohort. From the labour-market angle, this is a micro-optimization: it makes entry to the selective track slightly more merit-transparent for the admitted slice, but does nothing for the 88–92% majority who remain in standard-track schools with lower peer quality, lower investment, and lower post-secondary progression rates to high-skill roles. The employer interest is in a large, diverse, early-identified talent base across ALL secondary pathways — not just a cleaner selection into a narrow gate. The tech sector in particular has invested in secondary partnerships and apprenticeship pipelines in general schools; S1 does not strengthen those. The lottery mechanism is weak-evidenced and does not address the core issue: that the track structure itself sorts by family capital. Condition: S1 becomes relevant to the employer interest only if paired with demonstrable investment in general-school advanced programmes and documented employer-school partnership growth in those schools; without that, it is institutional hygiene, not labour-market reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: Evidence that (a) standardised admission measurably expands employer access to high-ability low-SES candidates in the selected track, AND (b) general-school advanced programmes are resourced and partnered with employers such that non-selected high-ability pupils have credible post-secondary pathways to skill roles. Without (b), the value of a cleaner selection into a narrow pipe is marginal.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The employer and tech-sector interest in a broader, more diverse secondary-to-tertiary-to-work pipeline; the public-good frame is system-wide human-capital development and reduced skills-matching friction through exposure of a larger cohort to advanced, collaborative, heterogeneous peer environments earlier in their schooling.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector who recruit from secondary and tertiary education and depend on candidates with collaborative problem-solving, diverse-team experience, and exposure to mixed-ability peer groups.
reactions.public_good_frame: A gradually expanding pool of students exposed to advanced-programme secondary education in mixed-ability environments develops collaborative, adaptive skills and reduces the information asymmetries that plague school-to-work transitions; employers gain access to a larger cohort of analytically capable, team-experienced candidates from diverse backgrounds.
reactions.argument: S2 directly addresses the labour-market constraint that the current 6/8-year system creates: a small, SES-skewed pool of track-admitted candidates and a residual general-school majority with lower exposure to advanced peers and employers. The phase-down mechanism is gradual, preserving existing-cohort continuity (felmenő rendszer) and reducing the political instability that could otherwise trigger reversal. The explicit commitment to advanced in-school programmes is critical: if those programmes are resourced and partnered with employers (apprenticeships, project-based learning, mentorship), the freed capacity becomes a labour-market asset rather than a cost. The weak point is employer confidence that these programmes will actually exist and be sustained: the evidence base is US-specific and untested in Hungary. The phased approach itself is an advantage from a risk perspective — it allows course correction. However, the condition is severe: unless the multi-party pact in step 1 is real and credible, and unless non-state selective caps are binding (step 4), the phase-down simply creates incentive for private-school migration, leaving the state general-school majority worse off. The tech sector in particular has scale-advantage in partnerships with large general-school systems; fragmentation into selective private/church schools reduces that leverage. Condition: the legislative lock (step 1) must be perceived as genuinely durable, and non-state caps must bind; without both, S2 is a transition to increased stratification, not reduced.
reactions.condition_to_change: Evidence that (a) the multi-party pact survives at least one electoral cycle without reversal, (b) non-state selective intake does not expand to absorb the freed state capacity, (c) advanced programmes in general schools are resourced and measurably partnered with employers for apprenticeships/work-integrated learning, and (d) employer survey data show confidence in the skill-readiness of non-track-admitted graduates from advanced programmes. If (a) and (b) fail, S2 becomes S4-under-disguise.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The employer and tech-sector interest in a large, cognitively diverse, collaboratively experienced secondary cohort; the public-good frame is system-wide human-capital development, reduced skills-matching friction, and preparation of the entire cohort for adaptable, team-based, cross-functional work environments.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector who depend on graduates with demonstrated ability to work in cognitively diverse teams, adapt to rapid skill requirements, and bring heterogeneous problem-solving approaches.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection to age 14 and ensuring mixed-ability comprehensive secondary education maximises the proportion of the cohort exposed to collaborative, high-performing peers and varied learning approaches; this builds the adaptive, team-capable workforce that modern digital and knowledge economies require.
reactions.argument: S3 is the strongest match between labour-market interests and structural reform. The tech and digital sectors operate on the assumption that innovation and problem-solving benefit from cognitive diversity, not homogeneous high-performers. The employer interest is not in a narrow elite — it is in a large, internally diverse, well-prepared cohort whose members can work together across different expertise areas. S3 delivers that by keeping the full cohort together through age 14, exposing all to mixed-ability peer groups and requiring teachers to support differentiation rather than sorting. The asymmetric-peer-effect assumption is load-bearing, but it aligns with employer observation: non-selected students gain problem-solving exposure and confidence from high-achieving peers, while high-achievers develop collaborative and communication skills from explaining concepts across ability levels. The concern is real — if peers effects are symmetric (zero-sum), S3 is redistribution without creation, and middle-class exit becomes likely. But the employer interest in that scenario is still with the comprehensive outcome: even if some high-SES families exit to private schools, if the state system serves 70–80% of the cohort with cognitively diverse, team-capable graduates, the labour-market value is higher than the current system where the top track is narrow and peers in general schools are homogeneously lower-SES and lower-ability. The key risk is middle-class exit past a threshold (S3 itself proposes two-thirds as a decision gate). The implementation steps are demanding: teacher supply, differentiation capacity, concurrent choice-and-catchment reform. However, the labour-market payoff, if the reform holds at a reasonable exit threshold, is substantial — a cohort of 100,000 with internal diversity is more valuable than a cohort of 20,000 elite plus 80,000 residual. Condition: middle-class exit must not exceed the two-thirds gate (i.e., top-SES-quartile enrolment in state general schools stays ≥two-thirds of pre-reform level); if it does, the system has inverted and become regressive.
reactions.condition_to_change: Evidence that (a) peer effects in mixed-ability Hungarian lower secondary are asymmetric (non-selected pupils gain without high-achievers losing), (b) teacher differentiation capacity can be built to a threshold where within-class variance does not exceed between-class variance by more than 50%, (c) non-state selective caps prevent demand migration above the middle-class-exit threshold, and (d) student outcome data at age 14 and post-secondary show no mean-performance loss and a measurable narrowing of the SES-outcome gradient. If (a) proves false or (b) cannot be scaled, S3 degrades toward redistributive regress.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The employer and tech-sector interest in system-wide human-capital development and cognitive diversity in the cohort; the public-good frame is labour-market efficiency and innovation capacity, which depend on broad secondary-to-work pipeline access and exposure of a large cohort to mixed-ability peer learning.
reactions.interest: Employers and the digital/tech sector who depend on a large, cognitively diverse, collaboratively experienced secondary-to-work pipeline.
reactions.public_good_frame: Efficient labour-market matching and innovation capacity require a secondary system that exposes a large, diverse cohort to mixed-ability collaboration and advanced problem-solving; compensation for a stratified system does not create that capacity and may entrench the very sorting that creates labour-market friction.
reactions.argument: S4 is incompatible with the labour-market interest because it accepts the stratification premise and attempts to compensate for it rather than remove it. From the employer perspective, this is economically wasteful: instead of building collaborative, diverse secondary cohorts, S4 invests heavily in raising the floor of a residual majority while leaving the track structure — the source of the problem — intact. The TEIP analogy is misleading in the Hungarian context for two reasons. First, Portugal applied TEIP within a relatively late-tracking (age 15) system; Hungary selects at 10–12, earlier in cognitive and social development, where compensation is weaker and sorting effects are stronger. Second, S4's core claim — that compensation can 'offset, not mask' segregation at early selection ages — is explicitly unverifiable ex ante (per the scenario's own evidence grade). This is not a minor caveat: it is the load-bearing assumption, and the scenario admits it has no evidence base at Hungary's selection age. The employer interest cannot rationally support a multi-year, high-cost intervention resting on an unverified assumption, particularly when the alternative (S2 or S3) removes the sorting mechanism itself. S4 also creates a perverse incentive: if investment in general schools can 'offset' segregation, there is no political pressure to remove the track structure, and the system becomes politically locked into ongoing compensation spending. From the tech sector's perspective, this is a permanent subsidy to an inefficient system architecture. Finally, S4 is politically fragile in exactly the way that matters to employers: it requires sustained, multi-year targeted funding in a sector that has no natural constituency in fiscal downturns. Employers have no leverage to protect the funding against budget cuts; the legislative lock that S2 proposes (step 1) does not exist in S4. The result is budget volatility and recurring uncertainty about whether the compensation mechanism will persist — a poor foundation for secondary-to-work partnerships. Condition: S4 becomes defensible only if (a) independent evidence emerges that compensation actually offsets segregation at early selection ages (not just in Portugal's late-tracking system), AND (b) a legislative mechanism exists to make targeted funding recession-proof at the 85–90% level that research suggests is the effective threshold. Without (a) and (b), S4 is costlier and riskier than S2 or S3.
reactions.condition_to_change: Publishable evidence (ideally from a quasi-experimental or natural-experiment design in a comparable early-tracking system) that non-structural compensation measurably offsets segregation outcomes at age 10–12 selection and that the offset persists when budgets decline by >20%. If that evidence emerges, and if a legislative lock protects targeted funding below −20% cuts, S4 becomes a defensible holding pattern pending structural reform.
